content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 20, 2017

Russell B. Blair
8371 Carrick Court
Citrus Heights, CA 95610

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-126

Dear Mr. Blair:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice provided assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Further, we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

As a Citrus Heights Planning Commissioner, will you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in governmental decisions regarding proposed development projects that were reviewed and commented on by Metro Fire, when Metro Fire has provided revenue to your employer.

CONCLUSION

NO. Any financial effect on your employer through the Planning Commission's decisions on the proposed development projects that have been reviewed and commented on by Metro Fire are neither foreseeable nor material.

FACTS

You are a member of the seven-person Planning Commission for the City of Citrus Heights. You also are employed on a part time basis by a municipal consulting firm – Interwest Consulting Group Inc ("Interwest"). Interwest is a 230-employee company that provides plan review and safety inspections on public projects in Colorado, California, and Nevada.² The company retains licensed

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Though you have not provided information on the amount of income you have received from Interwest in the past 12 months, we assume this amount is at least $500.

File No. A-17-126
Page No. 2

professional fire protection engineers that assess public entities' plans for compliance with
applicable fire code and standard requirements.

Although Interwest does not contract directly with the City, it has a contract with the
Sacramento Metropolitan Fire Protection District ("Metro Fire") for consulting services. As a
special district, Metro Fire contracts for fire protection services, fire suppression, inspection, plan
checking, emergency transportation and medical services, public education, advanced life support,
and rescue services with the cities of Citrus Heights and Rancho Cordova as well as the
communities of Antelope, Arden/Arcade, Carmichael, Elverta, Fair Oaks, Florin, Foothill Farms,
Gold River, Mather, McClellan, North Highlands, Orangevale, Rancho Murieta, Rio Linda, and
unincorporated areas of Sacramento County.

Metro Fire assesses proposed development projects for compliance with applicable water
flow and fire protection requirements as part of its plan check process for the City. You informed
me that the Planning Commission decides whether to approve these projects to which Metro Fire
has reviewed and commented. Interwest's contract with Metro Fire amounted to approximately 0.51
percent of Interwest's total revenue for 2016. Of that 0.51 percent, a much smaller percentage was
for work conducted within the City limits. You inquired whether you will have a disqualifying
conflict of interest in partaking in proposed development projects that Metro Fire has reviewed
given that Metro Fire provides income to Interwest, your employer.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Interests from
which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the following:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

According to your facts, you have a financial interest in Interwest as a source of income.
(Section 87103(c).) Section 87103 also indicates that an official has a financial interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of "his or her immediate family," also known as the "personal
financial effects" rule.³ You do not have an interest in Metro Fire.

Foreseeability:

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.

³ Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest
in a business entity. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further.

File No. A-17-126
Page No. 3

(Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Under the facts you have provided, Interwest is not explicitly involved in the decisions before the Planning Commission. Thus, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."⁴

Materiality – Business Entity as Source of Income:

Regulation 18702.1 provides the standard for materiality when the income is for services or a salary and the source is a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation 18702.1(b), an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business entity. The regulation also provides examples of decisions that may have a material effect on a business entity, including a decision that would increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies. (Regulation 18702.1(b)(3)).

You stated Interwest's contract with Metro Fire amounted to approximately 0.51 percent of Interwest's total revenue for 2016. Of that 0.51 percent, a much smaller percentage was for work conducted within the City limits. It is unlikely that such a small increase in the business entity's revenue would materially affect its value. Accordingly, the financial effect of the Planning Commission's decisions on your financial interest is not reasonably foreseeable or material.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

⁴ Regulation 18701(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the "reasonably foreseeable" determination.

were reviewed and commented on by Metro Fire, when Metro Fire has provided revenue to your 
employer.

the proposed development projects that have been reviewed and commented on by Metro Fire are 
neither foreseeable nor material.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or 
consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Interests from 
which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the following:

regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
1 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest 
in a business entity. (Regulation I 8702.5(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further.  
(Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Under the facts you have 
provided, Interwest is not explicitly involved in the decisions before the Planning Commission. 
Thus, Regulation 1870l(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."4 
Materiality - Business Entity as Source of Income: 
Regulation 18702.1 provides the standard for materiality when the income is for services or 
a salary and the source is a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation 
18702.1 (b ), an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it
